content.thinking:
Let me carefully analyze the diff provided for the `svf-vertical-slice` feature audit. I'll look at the three files in this chunk:

1. `specs/svf-vertical-slice/tasks.md` - Task completion status changes
2. `tests/CMakeLists.txt` - New test CMake configuration
3. `tests/core/no-allocation-test.cpp` - New allocation invariant test

Let me analyze each carefully for the categories of issues mentioned: correctness bugs, design issues, missed edge cases, code-quality concerns, cross-cutting impact, documentation drift, and operator-discipline traps.
Looking at the tasks file, I notice all tasks are being marked complete, but there's an inconsistency in the checkbox styles—the manual acceptance tasks use HTML entities rather than markdown checkboxes, which appears intentional as separate operator checkpoints. Task T027 was also modified from requiring a full end-to-end interactive scenario run to just a build-verification step, which represents a significant scope reduction that should be documented.

In the CMakeLists.txt file, the test target is linking against both the core library and the host library along with the doctest framework.

The test suite is set to use C++20, which might be stricter than necessary if the goal is verifying C++17 compatibility. The test file itself covers both the bare `SvfEffect` and the `EffectNode<SvfEffect>` wrapper from the host boundary, which explains why both libraries are needed.

Looking at the allocation test more closely, there's a potential issue with how the sentinel is being used. The test resets the sentinel before the loop, then constructs an `AudioBlock` inside each iteration and processes it while also changing parameters. I need to examine whether this setup is actually catching the allocations correctly. The second test is more focused and doesn't include `setParameter` calls, which might be intentional for testing just the processing path. I'm also noting that the thread-local sentinel approach works fine for synchronous unit tests, though it would miss allocations on other threads. The test references `SvfEffect::kCutoff` as a public constant, which seems reasonable for the parameter ID.

There's a potential issue with how the test measures allocations—it only resets the sentinel once before all 100 iterations, so if `process()` does any setup allocation on the first call, that would still violate the no-heap-allocation requirement even though subsequent calls might be clean. The test doesn't distinguish between first-call setup and steady-state behavior.

Actually, looking more carefully: `prepare()` is called before the reset, so any allocations there are excluded from measurement. This is the right approach since the spec allows `prepare()` to allocate—only `process()` must be allocation-free. The block-size loop structure in the first test creates a new context for each size, which...

The destructor runs after the sentinel capture ends, so any cleanup allocations wouldn't be measured anyway—correct for RT testing. Each iteration creates an `AudioBlock` on the stack as a view into existing data, so that's fine. But I'm noticing the test only exercises one parameter with `setParameter`, which might not be comprehensive enough.

The second test case covers `EffectNode::processBlock` but skips parameter setting through the node itself, so if the node's parameter-forwarding path allocates separately, that gap would go undetected. The `AllocationSentinel` is thread-local and works fine for synchronous testing, though any internal thread pools would be invisible. I should also consider whether testing with different channel counts beyond the current 2-channel setup would be valuable.

Looking at the CMakeLists configuration, the test compiles with C++20 and links against the core library, but I'm noticing the `Effect` concept in the header might have conditional compilation based on C++ standard support.

The real issue here is that the test suite always uses C++20, so the C++17 duck-typed fallback path never gets exercised. The spec requires both the concept and the fallback to work, but if tests only run with C++20, the C++17 path could be broken without anyone knowing. The C++17 fallback just removes the `requires` constraint and relies on duck typing, so runtime behavior stays the same between C++17 and C++20—the risk is purely compile-time constraint checking, which drops this to low severity. The second test case doesn't exercise `setParameter` through the `EffectNode` boundary, leaving that allocation path unchecked.

For the third finding, `AudioBlock` is constructed inside the measured region, but since it's supposed to be a non-owning view, this should be allocation-free—though any future changes adding a container to it would silently break the test in a confusing way.

Looking at the Tasks.md changes, T027/T031/T035 show scope reductions where acceptance testing is being split between automated verification (build and link) and manual interactive testing, but these changes aren't clearly flagged as scope reductions in the task definitions.

The manual acceptance section uses `☐` checkbox characters instead of standard markdown syntax (`- [ ]`), so they'll render as plain text rather than interactive checkboxes. More critically, the original task asked to "Run quickstart Scenario B end-to-end" but now excludes the actual interactive scenario run—the checkpoint shifted from "US1 is independently shippable" to "US1 build-complete." This honestly reflects what was actually accomplished versus what was planned, and the manual section does call this out, but from an audit perspective it raises the question of whether the original US1 acceptance scenarios were ever fully verified.

The pending manual acceptance gates are explicitly documented, which is good practice for honest bookkeeping, but it means the feature hasn't completed full acceptance testing yet. An auditor should note this as an informational finding—the split between automated build verification and pending manual acceptance is transparent, but the feature remains incomplete against its original acceptance criteria.

Now looking at the test setup for the allocation sentinel: the parameter changes are being applied after the process call in each iteration, which doesn't match a real-time scenario where parameter updates might come before processing and get applied during the next process call. This timing difference means the test isn't fully exercising the allocation behavior that would occur when parameters change right before audio processing. The test suite exercises parameter changes well across iterations, with 99 out of 100 `process()` calls happening after a `setParameter` call that modifies the cutoff value. However, there's a gap in C++17 portability verification—the test binary is forced to C++20, so the C++17 duck-typed fallback for `Effect` never gets exercised, even though the spec requires it as a foundational requirement.

Additionally, the manual acceptance section in tasks.md uses Unicode checkbox characters instead of markdown syntax, which means GitHub won't recognize them as interactive task list items for tracking completion.

Looking at the overall findings, there are a few issues worth flagging: the C++17 code path isn't being tested by the current test suite, the allocation test doesn't fully exercise parameter setting through the node interface, and the manual acceptance gates for the user stories represent a significant dependency that needs to be tracked separately. The `setParameter` method on `EffectNode` should also be tested for allocations if it touches the effect's parameter storage. I'm prioritizing my findings by impact: the C++17 fallback being untested is medium risk since CI won't catch regressions there, the `EffectNode` parameter coverage gap is lower priority without confirming the method exists, the checkbox formatting issue is minor, and the pending interactive acceptance is just informational.

Now I'm examining the test code more closely to see if there are any correctness issues in how the allocation testing is structured. Actually, `AllocationSentinel::reset()` is called inside the block size loop, not before it—so the sentinel gets reset for each iteration, which means we're measuring allocations separately for each block size configuration.

The `std::vector` allocations for the audio buffers and the `SvfEffect` constructor both happen before the reset, so they're correctly excluded from the measurement. The `prepare()` call is also before the reset, which is fine since setup allocations are allowed.

Now I'm wondering about the `ParamId{SvfEffect::kCutoff}` construction happening inside the measured loop—if that's just wrapping an integer constant, it should be a trivial operation with no allocations.

Looking at the findings, there are a couple of issues: the C++17 fallback code path in the effect header isn't being tested since the test suite compiles with C++20, so any regressions there would only surface during cross-compilation for MCU targets. Also, the `EffectNode` parameter path doesn't appear to be covered by the allocation test.

I'm also noticing some documentation issues—the manual acceptance section in the tasks file uses Unicode checkboxes instead of GitHub's standard task list syntax, so it won't integrate with GitHub's tracking system. And while the human-loop acceptance criteria for audio, DAW, and hardware testing are documented as pending, downstream users should be aware that interactive verification hasn't happened yet.

Let me think through the `setParameter` test more carefully. The test calls it with allocation-free parameter updates, but whether that method actually runs on the audio thread in production depends on the design—it could be called from the UI thread with a thread-safe queue instead. If the design has `setParameter` running directly in the audio callback and updating filter coefficients synchronously, then testing it alongside `process()` makes sense. But if there's a producer-consumer model where the UI thread enqueues changes and the audio thread applies them, the test is validating something different than what happens in practice.

That said, for an allocation-specific invariant test, this simplification is acceptable—we're just checking that the operation doesn't trigger heap allocation, not necessarily simulating the exact threading model.

Looking at the `tasks.md` changes, the redefinition of T027/T031/T035 from end-to-end validation to build-only verification is a bit concerning. The pattern of retroactively narrowing task scope to match what was actually accomplished rather than what was originally required is a subtle red flag, though the fact that it's done transparently with explicit documentation in the "Manual acceptance" section mitigates that somewhat.

Let me organize my findings: the C++17 fallback path isn't exercised by automated tests, the EffectNode parameter path is missing from the allocation test, the manual acceptance section uses non-standard checkbox syntax, and the task scope for T027/T031/T035 was narrowed post-hoc with interactive acceptance still pending. The core question is whether the original acceptance criteria were actually met or just redefined. I've got enough to compile my findings now.

content.text:
### C++17 duck-typed `Effect` fallback path never exercised by `ctest --preset test`

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-01
Status:     open
Severity:   medium
Surface:    tests/CMakeLists.txt:17

`tests/CMakeLists.txt` pins the test binary to C++20 (`target_compile_features(acfx_core_tests PRIVATE cxx_std_20)`). The `Effect` concept in `core/dsp/effect.h` has an `__cpp_concepts`-guarded C++17 duck-typed fallback (spec T011). Because the test binary always compiles at C++20, the fallback code path is never compiled or run by `ctest --preset test` or CI. A regression that breaks the C++17 fallback (a missing duck-typed method, an incompatible defaulted template, a C++20-only construct leaking into a `#else` branch) will not be caught by any automated test. The MCU cross-build (T034/T035) provides the only compile-time guard for C++17, but that path is absent from the CI test run and was itself marked "compile-verify only" (no firmware link or test execution). The blast-radius: a CI-green C++17 regression could silently ship and only surface when building for Daisy or Teensy.

A minimal fix is a second `add_executable(acfx_core_tests_cxx17 ...)` target in `tests/CMakeLists.txt` using `target_compile_features(... PRIVATE cxx_std_17)` with the same sources, registered to a separate CTest label, and included in the CI matrix under the `test` preset. Alternatively, an explicit note in `CONTRIBUTING.md` acknowledging the gap and committing to MCU-CI coverage as the only C++17 guard.

---

### `EffectNode<SvfEffect>` allocation test omits the node's `setParameter` path

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-02
Status:     open
Severity:   low
Surface:    tests/core/no-allocation-test.cpp:43–56

The second test case (`EffectNode<SvfEffect>::processBlock allocates nothing`) exercises only `node.processBlock(block)`. It never calls a `setParameter`-equivalent through the `EffectNode` boundary. If `EffectNode` (spec T019: "≤1 vcall/block") has a thread-safe parameter-delivery path — a lock-free queue entry, an atomic swap, or any forwarding mechanism — the allocation behaviour of that path is untested. The first test case (`SvfEffect::process`) explicitly tests `setParameter` under the comment "parameter changes on the audio thread must also be allocation-free," making the omission in the second case visible by contrast. Blast-radius: if the node's parameter path allocates on the audio thread, FR-014 is violated and the defect is invisible to the test suite and CI.

The fix is a single additional block in the second test case mirroring the first: call the node's parameter-setting method inside the measured loop after `processBlock`, and assert zero allocations.

---

### Manual-acceptance checkboxes use `☐` (Unicode) not GitHub task-list syntax

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-03
Status:     open
Severity:   low
Surface:    specs/svf-vertical-slice/tasks.md (new "Manual acceptance" section, lines ~163–175)

The new "Manual acceptance" section uses the Unicode character `☐` as a checkbox. GitHub's task-list rendering only recognises `- [ ]` / `- [x]` (GFM spec §task-list-items) as interactive, trackable checkboxes. The `☐` bullets render as plain-text bullet points in all GitHub views (PR, issue, wiki); the item count will not appear in the sidebar, and the boxes cannot be ticked by clicking. All other tasks in the file use `- [X]` or `- [ ]` correctly. Blast-radius: the operator may not notice the pending acceptance items are untracked by GitHub's completion mechanism, and a future summary of "open checkboxes" (by tooling or by visual scan) will undercount the outstanding work.

---

### T027 / T031 / T035 definitions narrowed post-hoc; interactive acceptance remains unverified

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-04
Status:     open
Severity:   informational
Surface:    specs/svf-vertical-slice/tasks.md (T027, T031, T035 hunks)

Each of T027 ("run Scenario B end-to-end and confirm all US1 acceptance scenarios"), T031 ("run Scenario C and confirm all US2 acceptance scenarios"), and T035 ("run Scenario D: build + link artifacts…") was redefined in this commit to match what was actually accomplished (build + automated verification) rather than what the original task required (interactive live run / DAW instantiation / flashed firmware). The honest "Manual acceptance" section is a positive addition, but the effect of the rewrite is that all tasks are now marked `[X]` complete while the original acceptance criteria for three of them remain unmet and are captured only as freeform `☐` operator checkpoints. A downstream consumer reading the task list as "all [X] means the feature is verified" would draw the wrong conclusion.

This is documented in good faith and the pending items are visible in context, but the pattern of retroactively narrowing task definitions to match execution-scope is worth noting: the feature's user-facing behaviour (audio sweep, DAW parity, flashed hardware) has not been verified end-to-end by any automated or manual run captured in this commit.
